artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Finally catching my breath after what feels like the longest few months. I wanted to write everything down for you instead of trying to cram it into a quick phone call between dinner and piano lessons.

First, Evan. You know how wound up he’s been since winter break. In late February we took him up to OHSU Doernbecher Children’s Hospital in Portland, and they officially diagnosed him with an anxiety disorder. It was a hard day, but weirdly also a relief to have a name for what we’ve been seeing. His child psychiatrist, Dr. Melissa Nguyen, has been great so far. At our March 3, 2025 follow‑up she actually encouraged us to keep him in Destination Imagination and lean into the Quantum Quokkas’ microscope project as a kind of structured coping strategy.

Dr. Nguyen said the way he settles when he’s debugging the Arduino timing and talking through gear ratios with his teammates is basically informal exposure therapy for his worries. Evan comes home from Highland Park Middle School’s DI practices calmer than from regular school days. The other kids have really rallied around him these past six weeks, especially when he gets stuck in his “what if I mess up” loops.

On top of that, we’re still juggling Ryan’s stuff and the usual work craziness at LuminaBio, so DI has turned into this odd little island of sanity for all of us. I’ll try to drive down to Salem with Maya next Sunday for a proper “grandma day” if the weather cooperates.

Love you,
Heather

Hey Kara,

I owe you about twelve updates, so here’s the brain dump while the kids argue about whose turn it is to walk Daisy.

You already know about Ryan being on partial medical leave from Sunset last fall, but I don’t think I told you how much Evan’s DI team basically adopted us. From October through December 2024, while Ryan was in the outpatient program for major depressive disorder at Providence St. Vincent Medical Center, the Quantum Quokkas started organizing extra Sunday build sessions at our place on Aldercrest Lane.

They did it so Evan wouldn’t fall behind on the scanning microscope build when I was driving Ryan to his twice‑weekly group therapy sessions. Picture six middle schoolers in our dining room, 3D‑printed gears everywhere, while I’m checking the clock to make sure I can get Ryan across town in time. They’d show up with their own snacks, run motor tests, tweak the LED sequences, and text me questions if they hit something they couldn’t solve without me.

Fast‑forward to January’s ice storm: power out here for 48 hours, and we were already staring at a past‑due $640 PGE bill plus the $275 DI regional tournament fee. Without me even hinting, the kids decided to redesign the microscope to run on separate AA battery packs instead of the benchtop supply at my LuminaBio lab. They said, “We don’t want to stress your work or your bills.” Middle schoolers. I cried in the pantry.

Between that, Dad’s health stuff in Salem, and my own near‑PIP drama at LuminaBio last spring, I honestly don’t know how we’d be holding it together without this little herd of nerds.

Call when you can. I miss you.

– H

metadata.subject: Why Evan’s DI team means so much right now

artifacts.content:
Title: Evan – therapy + DI notes for next Dr. Nguyen visit

Date: 2025-03-02

Things to remember for 3/3/25 follow‑up at OHSU Doernbecher (child psychiatry – Dr. Melissa Nguyen):

– Late February diagnosis: Evan officially labeled with generalized anxiety. Triggers we’ve noticed since then: tests at Highland Park Middle School, loud group work, and unstructured time at home.
– DI as coping: When he’s working with the Quantum Quokkas on the microscope (especially debugging the Arduino timing loops and checking the motor steps against their engineering notebook), he visibly relaxes. Should describe this to Dr. Nguyen as “informal exposure therapy” she mentioned.
– Social support: For almost six weeks after the diagnosis, the team’s practices at Highland Park have been his safest space. Kids check in with him when he looks overwhelmed instead of teasing.

Other overlapping stressors to mention:

– Ryan: Still in maintenance phase after the Providence St. Vincent outpatient program for major depressive disorder (partial medical leave from Sunset HS Oct–Dec 2024). Twice‑weekly group therapy is over, but he’s wiped out by the end of each school day.
– Money: January 2025 ice storm, 48 hours without power, $640 past‑due PGE bill, plus scraping together the $275 DI regional fee. Quokkas’ decision to move microscope to AA battery packs so we don’t depend on LuminaBio’s lab power.
– My work: May 2024 performance improvement plan at LuminaBio after two missed prototype deadlines (too many late‑evening DI test sessions). Need to be honest about burnout and how that affects my patience with Evan.

Goal: Ask Dr. Nguyen for concrete strategies we can use during DI build nights when Evan starts spiraling but still wants to stay with the team.

Event: Drive Ryan to St. Vincent – group therapy

Date: Every Tuesday & Thursday, 2024-10-08 to 2024-12-19
Time: 5:15–7:30 p.m.
Location: Providence St. Vincent Medical Center, Outpatient Behavioral Health
Attendees: Heather, Ryan

Notes:
– Ryan’s partial medical leave from Sunset High School this fall; twice‑weekly group therapy sessions for major depressive disorder.
– Leave Beaverton by 4:45 p.m. to beat traffic from Aldercrest Lane.
– Coordinate with Quantum Quokkas families so Sunday build sessions at our house cover anything Evan misses during weeknight practices.
– Remind Evan: it’s okay if he can’t make every DI meeting; team already offered to do extra microscope work here on Sundays.

Related events/reminders:
– “Quokkas at our house – microscope build” – Sundays 2–5 p.m. (dining room). Make sure 3D printer is set up and Daisy is walked beforehand.
– Check in with LuminaBio calendar so I don’t book late prototype tests on group nights; still feeling raw after May PIP for missing two stage‑redesign deadlines.
– Budget check: gas for hospital trips + setting aside money for January 2025 DI regional tournament fee ($275) so we don’t repeat the panic we had during the ice storm and the $640 PGE bill.

To bring:
– Ryan’s notebook, updated meds list, snack, water.
– My laptop so I can review sample stage CAD or Quantum Quokkas’ engineering notebook in the waiting room if brain space allows.

metadata.title: Drive Ryan to St. Vincent – group therapy
metadata.location: Providence St. Vincent Medical Center – Outpatient Behavioral Health

artifacts.content:
Hi Elena,

I wanted to follow up on our May 22, 2024 1:1 and give you a bit more context now that I’ve had time to breathe.

Being on a formal performance improvement plan after missing those two prototype deadlines was a wake‑up call. I underestimated how much the late‑evening test sessions I was running for my son’s Destination Imagination team were bleeding into my responsibilities on the motorized sample stage redesign. I’ve since told the Quantum Quokkas (and Evan) that we can’t use the LuminaBio lab as our default after‑hours playground anymore.

To their credit, the kids responded in a way that honestly humbled me. They reorganized their DI tasks so they do most of the mechanical and coding work at Highland Park Middle School or at our dining room table. They’ve cut back on last‑minute lab access requests, and they’re now tracking microscope changes in their engineering notebook so I can review quickly instead of hovering for three hours.

The January 2025 ice storm drove this home even more. Our power was shut off for 48 hours, and with a $640 past‑due PGE bill plus the $275 DI regional tournament fee looming, I told them we couldn’t keep hauling the microscope into our lab to use the benchtop supply. They independently decided to redesign the system to run on separate AA battery packs, complete with a little distribution board and common ground. It was a nice reminder that mentoring doesn’t have to mean sacrificing work boundaries.

I’m committed to hitting the remaining PIP milestones on the stage project and keeping my evenings here focused on LuminaBio work (and sleep). Thanks again for the direct but kind feedback.

Best,
Heather

metadata.subject: Follow‑up on PIP and after‑hours boundaries

artifacts.content:
Hey Kara,

Quick update before I crash. It’s been a wild March.

Dad had a mild stroke in Salem on March 9, 2025. He’s at Salem Hospital and doing better than I feared, but there’s a lot of back‑and‑forth. I’ve spent the last three weekends shuttling between the hospital and his house, sorting meds, arguing with insurance, and starting the power‑of‑attorney conversations he’s been dodging for years.

The only reason I haven’t completely fallen apart is, again, the Quantum Quokkas. When they realized how much time Evan and I were going to be in Salem, they reworked their rehearsal schedule. Last Saturday they actually moved a full technical run‑through of the microscope and LED sequences to the community room at the Salem Public Library.

Picture this: I’m at a side table with Dad’s discharge paperwork spread out, on hold with a billing office, while six middle schoolers run a confocal‑microscope‑that‑isn’t‑really‑a‑microscope off AA battery packs ten feet away. They had the rotating sample stage, the RGB LED strip behind frosted acrylic, the whole thing. They even timed their run so Evan could be there between hospital visiting hours.

This is the same team that, back in October–December when Ryan was in the Providence St. Vincent outpatient program for major depressive disorder and on partial leave from Sunset, started coming over on Sundays so Evan wouldn’t miss build milestones while I drove Ryan to his twice‑weekly group sessions. And the same crew that redesigned the power system during the January ice storm when our PGE bill was $640 past due and we were scraping together the $275 for regionals.

I keep joking they’re raising us as much as we’re mentoring them.

Love,
Heather

metadata.subject: Dad, DI, and my second home on I‑5

artifacts.content:
Event: Drive to Salem – Dad’s discharge + DI rehearsal

Date: 2025-03-22
Time: 9:00 a.m.–6:30 p.m.
Location: Salem Hospital + Salem Public Library community room
Attendees: Heather, Evan, Daniel (for discharge portion), Quantum Quokkas (for rehearsal)

Agenda:

9:00–11:30 a.m. – Salem Hospital
– Meet with discharge nurse re: Dad’s mild stroke (3/9/25) and home care plan.
– Review meds list, PT schedule, and any paperwork related to power‑of‑attorney discussions.
– Call Mom from the cafeteria so she can ask questions.

12:00–4:00 p.m. – Salem Public Library community room
– Quantum Quokkas full technical run‑through of DI Scientific Challenge: Up Close.
– Set up rotating sample stage, Arduino, and RGB LED strip (AA battery packs only; no benchtop supply like at LuminaBio).
– Evan to run through his lines twice; watch for anxiety spikes and use breathing exercises from Dr. Nguyen at OHSU Doernbecher.
– Take photos/video for kids to review before regional tournament (remember $275 fee already paid, don’t stress about it today).

4:30–6:30 p.m. – Dad’s house
– Stock fridge, set up pill organizer, quick walkthrough of safety stuff.
– Leave by 6:30 to get Evan home at a reasonable hour for school at Highland Park on Monday.

Reminders:
– Pack engineering notebook, power‑strip, spare AA batteries, snacks.
– Bring my laptop in case there’s downtime to peek at LuminaBio emails (still on thin ice after May 2024 PIP).
